coredata.dc:title: Microrecycled Co3O4 from waste lithium-ion battery: Synthesis, characterisation and implication in environmental application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hossain, Rumana
Sahajwalla, Veena
coredata.dc:description: Microrecycling based techniques are developed to synthesise phase pure porous Co3O4 using thermal isolation and controlled recycling pathways to achieve environmental outcomes that could manufacture and recycle hazardous spent Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The existing synthesising strategies for high surface area and small crystal grains of Co3O4 rely on raw materials, complex chemicals and experimental conditions. Herein the proposed facile technique produces Co3O4 with nanosized crystal grains and a large specific surface area of 79.2 m2 g−1 by utilising the cathode and anode material (CAM) of spent LIBs. The controllable synthesis of the Co3O4 also offers value added co-product of Lithium in the form of Li2CO3. Further utilisation of synthesised Co3O4 as a catalyst for toxic dye degradation offers an excellent opportunity to deliver benefits for society by creating a resource from waste that could treat other waste in our environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste lithium-ion battery
Microrecycling
Cobalt recovery
Dye degradation
Value added material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterisation of the microrecycled Co3O4 nanoparticles
Effect of the parameters for the photocatalytic activity

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Australian Research Council’s Industrial Transformation Research Hub funding scheme (project IH190100009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a)TGA and DTG curve of the CAM with and without NaOH from 30° to 1000°C. (b) FTIR gas analysis of the CAM with and without NaOH in CAM during the thermal transformations.

(a) XRD diffraction pattern is showing the influence of temperature in the XRD pattern and (b) corresponding leaching rate of the Li co product. (c) XRD diffraction pattern of the influence of amount of catalyst used and (d) corresponding leaching rate.
Flow diagram of the synthesising process of the Co3O4 NPs and co-product of Li2CO3 from spent LIBS.
(a) XRD diffraction pattern; (b) XPS survey spectra; (c)high resolution XPS peaks of Co2p (d) N2 absorption/ desorption isotherms and (e) pore size distribution of the synthesised Co3O4.

(a) TEM image showing the morphology of the Co3O4 nanoparticles; (b) corresponding SAED pattern; (c) High-resolution image showing the lattice structure of a single crystal; (d) bright-field image of Co3O4; (e) & (f) is the EDS mapping for the BF showing the distribution of the Co and O simultaneously.
(a) Optical absorption properties at a wavelength range of 350–900 nm for the microrecycled Co3O4 (b) Direct and indirect optical transition plot for the bandgaps.

(a) Degradation of MBD by synthesised Co3O4 up to for 100 min; (b) degradation efficiency of Co3O4 up to 10 cycles; (c) degradation percentage doe different cycle after 100 min; (d) BET surface area and average pore size of the Co3O4 after different cycles.
Photocatalyst activity of the Co3O4. (a) effect of different concentrations of Co3O4; (b) different concentrations of MBD; (c)pH concentration and the (d) degradation kinetics.
Cyclic performance of the synthesised Co3O4 catalyst.
Comparative performance of the efficacy of synthesised catalyst in this study with the other relevant research.

head.ce:title: Microrecycled Co3O4 from waste lithium-ion battery: Synthesis, characterisation and implication in environmental application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Sustainable Materials Research and Technology, SMaRT@UNSW, School of Materials Science and Engineering, UNSW, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Sustainable Materials Research and Technology, SMaRT@UNSW, School of Materials Science and Engineering, UNSW

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Sustainable Materials Research and Technology, SMaRT@UNSW, School of Materials Science and Engineering, UNSW Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microrecycling based techniques are developed to synthesise phase pure porous Co3O4 using thermal isolation and controlled recycling pathways to achieve environmental outcomes that could manufacture and recycle hazardous spent Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The existing synthesising strategies for high surface area and small crystal grains of Co3O4 rely on raw materials, complex chemicals and experimental conditions. Herein the proposed facile technique produces Co3O4 with nanosized crystal grains and a large specific surface area of 79.2 m2 g−1 by utilising the cathode and anode material (CAM) of spent LIBs. The controllable synthesis of the Co3O4 also offers value added co-product of Lithium in the form of Li2CO3. Further utilisation of synthesised Co3O4 as a catalyst for toxic dye degradation offers an excellent opportunity to deliver benefits for society by creating a resource from waste that could treat other waste in our environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data and research materials will be available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has a wide range of application in electric vehicles, portable devices, and energy storage systems as they have excellent properties including low self-discharge, high voltage, high energy density, and have no memory effect compared with many other batteries [1]. Due to this, the consumption of LIBs is increasing day by day which further increases the generation of spent LIBs. These LIBs consist of hazardous components as well as valuable metals into it [2]. This makes it difficult to recover the valuable materials from LIBs [3]. However, the cathode active material present in the LIBs is associated with many valuable materials such as Li, Co, Mn, Al, Ni, etc., which draws huge attention [4,5].

Manufacturing challenges are expected to increase in the coming decades, due to global material and energy constraints and growing environmental issues caused by the increasing worldwide generation of waste. To address these difficulties, we need robust new technologies that are cost-effective, energy-efficient, and designed to minimise environmental pollution. To do this, the industry needs to adopt systems that eliminate waste where possible, recycle and reuse products, and transform unavoidable complex waste sources into valuable feedstock for manufacturing processes to conserve primary resources and the natural environment. The innovative concept of small-scale ‘microrecycling’ technologies can transform problematic waste where the recycling process is not relying on a large volume of waste as raw materials to operate a system [6]. Microrecycling recognises the value embedded in the problematic multi material waste and focuses on the selective recovery of the materials in a customised form that is fit for purpose [7,8]. For example, conventional smelting used for the copper recovery from the waste PCB does the recognise the value of other metal alloys and compounds. Where, microrecycling technique could be used to selectively recover the Cu and Sn-based alloys and valuable carbons separately [9,10]. Microrecycling is focused to produce the material with high value economically so that the feasibility of the process is dependent on the volume of the end product [11–13]. In the microrecycling process, the process parameters are selected carefully to transform the waste into products that could be the feedstock for another industrial application.

To make the recycling process environmentally friendly, a traditional method using organic acids as solutions to dissolve the active materials is used because of their self-degradation property and their advantage of higher leaching selectivity capability on the cathode materials of the LIBs for extracting the valuable materials [14]. However, those have limitations due to the higher cost of the application [15]. Alternatively, mineral acids are widely chosen because of their lower cost of the application and high capability of combining with the materials in the separation and purification processes with high leaching properties [2]. In process of separation, cobalt, lithium, and other transition metals are dissolved into the acid solutions, and dispersed into different flows in the process, causing a loss of a large quantity of lithium. By introducing a few methods in the recycling process utilising nitric acid [16], Phosphoric acid [17], and selective leaching and precipitation [18], the dispersion of Li is controlled. Followingly reductive agents such as hydrogen peroxide [19], sodium bisulfite [20], and some active metals [21], or organic reductants [22] are added to complete the acid leaching process. In the thermal transformation process, volatilisation or degradation plays a major role as a disadvantage to the process of carbothermic reduction process at 750 °C, reflecting a significant loss in mass of Li2CO3 [23]. The volatilisation in the carbothermic reaction procedure of cathode material causes a lithium loss of 30% Li at 750 °C [24]. A wide gap lies in between reality and ideal operating temperature, as well as considerations of the solubility of Li2CO3 that limits Li extraction using carbothermic reaction.

Metal hydroxides, such as KOH, NaOH, etc. are majorly used in the thermal transformation process to reduce the temperature for the carbonisation in the carbothermal reaction [25]. In the previous study, the high catalytic activity of the KOH and NaOH is observed which helps in a significant drop in temperature around (150–200 K) [26]. Using the catalytic reaction of the KOH or NaOH, LiCoO2 can undergo a reduction process at a lower operating temperature. At milder reaction conditions, Li and Co can be extracted as Li2O and CoO, as the reductants have an accurate control. After carbothermic reaction, the residual CoO could be obtained as a valuable product. Apart from obtaining the CoO, the water leaching of LiOH generates a higher concentration of Li as a valuable co-product. Therefore, a sustainable recycling process applying a controllable catalytic thermal transformation results in the production of a high concentration Li solution at a much lower and an easy process of recovering CoO. CoO was further thermally transformed to Co3O4 chemical state and was evaluated as the catalyst for dye degradation.

Nowadays the attraction of products is more dependent on the aesthetics of the products. Focusing on the colour of the products using various dye components in printing, food, textiles, and dyeing produces a large toxic effect globally. These dyes are non-biodegradable and toxic to the environment by affecting the natural growth process of the lives underwater and preventing the eutrophication processes [27]. Few industries producing these dye compounds are letting the residue into water bodies that contains carcinogen agents in it affecting the ecosystem. The concentrated dye is dissolved into water bodies and causes a strong colour to the stream, which further affects the penetration of sunlight which affects the aquatic life of the organisms [28]. In this research study, spent LIBs are processed for obtaining Co3O4 catalyst for the photodegradation process of methyl blue dye (MBD) which is an anionic triphenylmethane dye used in the dyeing process of cotton, fibers, and leathers and also used as a bacteriological and biological stain. Consumption of this dye causes skin and eye irritation and affects the respiratory [29].

In recent years, researchers are developing different technologies to synthesis Co3O4 nanostructures from waste LIBs by utilising the leaching solutions containing nitric acid [30], electrodeposition process in the presence of magnetic field [31], electrochemical precipitation [32], etc. However, several concerns are still present in the current processes which have extensive use of chemicals, high energy consumption, and complex multi-step separation processes [33]. While the recovery of the cobalt from the LIBs waste has been studied extensively, studies on synthesis of Co3O4 nanoparticles which will fit for another valuable application are still limited [30]. Co3O4 nanoparticles have tunable properties such as optical, magnetic, and electrochemical characteristics, and are widely used as an enhancer in electronic devices [23], heterogenous catalysts [21], and solid-state sensors [34]. Considering one of the most hazardous waste, spent LIBs can be converted into a useful form for environmental and economic purposes such as materials like photocatalyst [35], which would be a win-win situation for both manufacturing and our environment.

The spent LIBs used in this study were provided by a battery recycling centre. In the battery, the LiCoO2 cathode powder was pasted on Al foil, and the anode carbon powder (graphite) was pasted on copper foil. Iron shell, electrolyte, and separator membrane were also contained in the LIBs. The binder was polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The battery was fully discharged using the sodium chloride solution (5 wt% NaCl) and vacuum dried. The industrial scale crusher was used to crush the batteries and the crushed materials were received as it is. After the crushing, a sieving facility was used to separate different sized particles and the powders. The composition and size of the materials after sieving have been listed in Table 1S. The powder that is less than 700 µm particle size comprises cathode and anode materials (CAM) mainly with some minor impurities.

After the characterisation of the CAM powder, the thermal transformation was carried out in an oxygen-free environment under the Ar gas atmosphere from 400° to 750°C. The reason to select this temperature range is to establish an optimal microrecycling [6] temperature to prevent valuable metal loss as volatiles. The off-gas from the organic binder was condensed, collected, and analysed further. To keep the cost of the microrecycling process low, water was used as a leaching agent. After the thermal transformation, Li-based co-product was dissolved in water and recovered by precipitation and evaporation. The leaching was carried out for up to 30 min. After the extraction of the Li-based product, the Co compounds were recovered first as CoO with the remaining graphite. By utilising the thermal transformation of the CoO and graphite, phase pure Co3O4 was produced. The schematic of the furnace used for the thermal transformation of CAM is provided in Fig. S1.

The compositional analysis of the raw material and the products were determined by the inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (ICP-AES). The thermal transformation and the degradation mechanism were identified by the thermogravimetric (TGA) analysis (STA 8000, PerkinElmer). Phases of the materials were determined by the PANalytical X′Pert Pro multipurpose X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument using cobalt source radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). X′Pert High Score Plus software was used to identify the phases of the materials by analysing the collected XRD pattern. The materials’ composition was further confirmed with X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis carried out in a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250Xi machine, ulitising an Al Kα X-ray source. Volatile materials released from the thermal transformation of CAM with and without the catalyst were assessed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) gas analyser coupled with the TGA. The microscopical study was conducted by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The surface morphology and the surface area analysis were performed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis ulitising the N2 adsorption/desorption process at 77 K. and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method was used to identify the pore size distribution.

Under the irradiation of the visible light, the photocatalytic activity for the degradation of MBD of the microrecycled Co3O4 recovered from spent LIBs is derived. Photocatalyst (5 mg/20 mL) was finely dissolved in the aqueous solution of the MBD (20 mL, 50 mg/mL) was irradiated under visible light and was removed periodically for analysis for the experimental process to obtain the photocatalytic degradation. The solid catalyst particles were removed using centrifugation process on the aqueous solution.

After removing the bigger particles from the crashed spent lithium-ion battery waste (which has plastics, copper, and aluminium foils from the current collectors, casing, etc.) by sieving (Table S1), the black mass is recovered which is designated as cathode anode materials (CAM). In CAM, the cathode active material powder is present at around 60% and anode material, which is carbon/graphite, is present at around 38%. The rest 2% is the other materials such as copper and aluminium foils from the current collectors. ICP-AES (shown in Table S2) result demonstrates that the CAM has around 7% Li, 54% Co, 38% and the rest of the trace elements are Cu, Al, Fe, Mn, Ni, etc.

TGA and DTG curve in Fig. 1 shows that the weight loss did not take place until 720 °C in case of the thermal transformation of CAM without NaOH catalyst. However, from the literature, it is well understood that the carbothermic reaction can take place at 600 °C when the graphite is present in the system with the LiCoO2 [36]. At the initial state, carbon reacts with LiCoO2 and releases Li2O, CoO, and CO2 compound. As mentioned above, the reaction should take place at around 600 °C, however, no weight loss was observed till 720 °C. This phenomenon can be ascribed as the CO2 absorption of the Li2O compound and conversion into the Li2CO3 compound which is described in Eq. (4). It happens because the generated Li2O is double in comparison of the CO2 compound. In the beginning, the reduction product was CoO, Li2O, and CO2, however, Li2O tends to absorb CO2 to form Li2CO3, hence negligible weight loss occurred. An abrupt weight loss occurred when the temperature crosses 720 °C due to the release of the volatile materials of CO2 and Li2CO3 (as gasified at 720 °C). CO2 is further reduced by the solid C and generated CO gas. For this reason, the generation of CO2 starts earlier than the CO gas which is evident from the IR gas analyser.

When NaOH is added to the CAM, the TGA curve demonstrates a different pattern. It indicates that the gases are released at different temperature ranges. The initial weight loss up to 150 °C occurred due to the loss of H2O absorbed by the NaOH compound and then the emission of the water vapour. As is explained in the previous section, the reaction of carbon and LiCoO2 occurs way before the abrupt weight loss, so it is obvious that the reaction of carbon and LiCoO2 generated CO2 gas which was absorbed by Li2O. The generated CO2 reduced the NaOH and H2O released in the gaseous form which is described in Eq. 7. At this point, the weight loss occurred at 495 °C which is evident from the TGA and DTG curves (Fig. 1a & b).

When the temperature rises to 620 °C, the main reduction of LiCoO2 takes place and CoO was generated, which happened earlier than the thermal transformation of CAM without the presence of a catalyst (at 720 °C). A slight weight loss after 650 °C was observed which might be occurred due to the volatilisation of the Na2CO3. This result indicates that using the NaOH catalyst can reduce the carbothermic reaction temperature to a much lower temperature which can reduce the production cost of recycling. These phenomena are described from Eq. (1) to Eq. (7).
(1) C + 4LiCoO2→2Li2O + 4CoO + CO2
(2) 2Co3O4 + C→6CoO + CO2
(3) Li2O + CO2→Li2CO3
(4) 2CoO + C→2Co + CO2
(5) 3 C + 4LiCoO2→2Li2CO3 + 4Co + CO2
(6) CO2 + C → 2CO
(7) 2NaOH + CO2 →Na2CO3 + H2O

The volatile materials released from the thermal transformation of CAM with and without the catalyst were assessed by an FTIR gas analyser coupled with the TGA. The spectrum has been shown for the temperatures 120, 420, 520, 620, and 720 °C in Fig. 1c and d. From the spectrum, it is visible that the volatile compounds are similar that have been released from the system for both the cases. However, there are significant differences in the time of release. When the thermal transformation was carried out without the catalyst, the transformation of the compounds starts later compared to the CAM with catalyst. The significant transformation observed at 720 °C in the case of the CAM without the catalyst ((Fig. 1c)) is in line with the results described in Fig. 1a & b. This phenomenon is observed earlier when the NaOH catalyst was used (Fig. 1d). The absorption spectrums were visible in four significant regions of the wavelengths. The segment 4000–3200 cm−1 is attributed to the release of H2O and CO2 gases mainly [11]. The spectrum between the wavelength 2600–2000 cm−1 is for CO2 [11,37] . The spectrum between 2000 and 1200 cm−1 is corresponding to H2O, CO2, and CO [37].

The reduction and thermal transformation of the microrecycling process were carried out at 550 °C (after optimisation of the temperature described in the next section) in a horizontal tube furnace under the argon atmosphere and the IR gas analysis was carried out to identify the emission of CO, CO2, H2 gases. In this thermal transformation, significantly low temperature could perform the required carbothermal reduction due to the gasification of the carbon and the catalytic conversion. At the 550 °C molten NaOH catalyst attacks the graphitic surface and reacts with the C atoms. In this reaction, CO and H2 generate simultaneously which is also evident in the IR gas analysis curves. This phenomenon is also evident from the previous research [38,39]. The CO reduces LiCoO2 to the Li2O, CoO, and CO2 while the H2 reduces LiCoO2 to the Li2O, CoO, and H2O. The H2O is further absorbed by the Na2O which is left on the graphite surface and will form NaOH. These could be attributed to the following reactions (Eqs. 8– 12).
(8) 2NaOH + C → Na2O + CO + H2
(9) CO + 2LiCoO2→Li2O + 2CoO + CO2
(10) H2 + 2LiCoO2→Li2O + 2CoO + H2O
(11) Na2O + H2O → 2NaOH
(12) C + 4LiCoO2→2Li2O + 4CoO + CO2

The optimised temperature for the reduction reaction is assessed and for this, we have varied the temperature from 400° to 750°C for 1 hr ( Fig. 2a). At 400 °C, the diffraction peaks for the LiCoO2 are still dominant due to the insufficient temperature to complete the reaction. It is noteworthy that when the temperature is increased to 550 °C, the CoO peaks are visible which indicates the reduction of the CAM. The peaks of LiCoO2 disappear completely at this temperature, and the new peak only matches with the CoO phases and graphite. The corresponding water leaching rates of Li are justified by this result which shows only 80% for the sample generated at 500 °C and at 550 °C which is more than 90% (Fig. 2d). At the elevated temperature (~650 °C), new peaks are forming which are corresponding to the metallic Co. Therefore, the temperature of 550 °C has been considered the favourable temperature for this thermal transformation at which the concentration of the lithium is ~1.85 g·L–1, attributed to the 92% leaching rate.

For the optimisation of the amount of NaOH used as a catalyst, we have varied the wt. percentage of catalyst in the CAM powder from 5% to 40% (Fig. 2c). In Fig. 2a, it was revealed that when the catalyst used was in a range of 5–10%, LiCoO2 peaks are very prominent which is an indication of insufficient NaOH catalyst for the reaction. A very minor peak of LiCoO2 was detected for 15% catalyst used, above this range, in all the cases LiCoO2 was not detected and confirmed the carbothermic reduction completely. The leaching rate is increasing with the increasing percentage of NaOH catalyst. However, if the leaching rate is considered, it is observed that when the wt% of the NaOH is more than 20%, the leaching rate is reduced slightly which is attributed to the increase in the absorption of the CO2 in the NaOH and formation of more Na2CO3 which might have some negative effect on the metal leaching process (Fig. 2c). Moreover, without the catalyst the carbothermic reduction involved Li2O, Li2CO3, CoO, and Co when it is performed at a temperature lower than 600 °C, however, the reduction is always incomplete under this condition. If the temperature is increased, it triggers more formation of metallic Co and Li2CO3 and restricts Li2O which reduces the leaching rate. It is very difficult to optimise the process within a very short range of temperature for an efficient Li recovery. In this case, utilising the catalyst could widen the range of operating temperature for complete reduction of LiCoO2 and promotes the formation of Li2O which has better solubility in water compared to Li2CO3 [40].

The carbothermal transformation converted the CAM into CoO, Li2CO3, and residual graphite. The optimal parameters for this conversion are the addition of 20% NaOH catalyst and thermal transformation under the inert condition in Ar atmosphere at 550 °C for 1 h, and water leaching for 30 min at room temperature. This catalytic carbothermic transformation can generate Li2O, Li2CO3, and CoO from LiCoO2. After that, the selective leaching of Li was performed with 92% efficiency. During the water leaching process, more than 90% Li was recovered in this process. Li was recovered by precipitation methods. The washing residue was composed of CoO and graphite which was heat treated at 500 °C for 3 h in the air for the complete removal of the graphite and oxidising of the CoO to form pure Co3O4. The generated Co3O4 was further utilised to evaluate the suitability for MB degradation. The process flow is demonstrated in Fig. 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the microrecycled Co3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray powder diffraction of the recovered Co3O4 sample is represented in Fig. 4a. The diffraction pattern completely matches the peaks of Co3O4 with high purity. Using XPS analysis on Co3O4, the oxidation state is revealed and represented in Fig. 4b & c. the survey peaks revealed Co, O, and C peaks. The high-resolution Co peak revealed two peaks at 794.9 and 780.1 eV corresponding to the Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 spins orbital peaks of Co3O4. At 788 and 803 eV, two satellite peaks are located as a characteristic of Co3O4 similar to research reports in former literature [41]. The obtained results complement the high-resolution TEM analysis. The surface properties of the Co3O4 synthesised from LIBs were investigated by utilising N2 adsorption/desorption analysis at the temperature of 77 K (Fig. 4d). The pore-size distribution indicates a mesomicroporous structure with an average pore diameter of ~4 ± 0.5 nm ( Fig. 5e). The BET surface area of the material is calculated as 79.2 m2/g.

Fig. 5a represents the morphology and size of the nanoparticles of Co3O4. These particles are spherical, the average diameter for the particles is calculated in the range of 55–60 nm. Lattice diffraction (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) was obtained from the SAED pattern conferring the diffraction pattern standard by XRD. Polycrystalline samples expose a small construction of ring, that originates from Braggs reflection from the Co3O4 nanoparticle crystal. The obtained XRD and SAED pattern admits with the results obtained on crystalline Co3O4 nanoparticles. The interplanar spacing of the nanoparticles is obtained using high-resolution TEM image revealing the lattice fringes of 0.244 corresponding to the (311) planes of Co3O4 at face-centred cubic phase.

The optical absorption properties at a wavelength range of 350–900 nm were investigated for the microrecycled Co3O4. A graphical representation in Fig. 6 reveals the variations of absorbance (αt) at a wavelength (λ). From the figure it is clearly observed the decrease of wavelength up to ~600 nm of the absorbance and increases again up to ~750 nm and decreases again above this wavelength, representing two regions of the optical transitions (Fig. 6a).

Fig. 6b represents the plotting of two curves which attributes the presence of direct and indirect optical transition from the variations reflected in the plotting as straight lines in the lower and higher energy domains. The results obtained for both the direct optical band gap and indirect optical band gap energies are 2.10 and 1.60 eV, as researchers reports similar results when they fabricated the Co3O4 nanomaterials with raw resources, such as Co(NO3)2.6 H2O [42], Co(CO3)0.5(OH)·0.11H2O [43], Co(NO3)2 ⋅ 6H2O, CO(NH2)2 [44], Prussian blue analogue (PBA) Co3[Co(CN)6]2 [45], etc. and complicated synthesis steps with expensive chemicals [46]. Improvement in the photocatalytic activity of NPs can be achieved by decreasing the band gap values, resulting in raising an electron-hole formation under irradiation by visible light. The optical transition of the metal and the electronic structures are the main factors in accurately regulating the photocatalytic reaction. Fine adjusting the bang gap to the required stage and investigating the activity of the catalyst on the metal oxide semiconductor depends on the energy level of the material.

The photocatalytic performance of Co3O4 catalysts was evaluated through its degradation of MB (C16H18ClN3S) solution as a function of time. The concentration of the MB was measured by the relative intensity of the optical absorbance spectra.

The photocatalytic degradation process was carried out of Co3O4 in which an aqueous solution containing MB was placed under a visible light from a mercury lamp at 125 W. Due to irradiation of visible light the MB degrades for Co3O4 at 100 min time of light incidence which is due to the high surface area and surface defects, nanosized particle, lower bang gap, crystal structure/ phase transformed and the presence of bounding molecules on the surface [25]. In this case, a complex spinel structure develops for the cobalt oxide comprising Co3+ ions at octahedral sites and Co2+ ions at tetrahedral sites [47]. The photocatalytic oxidation process is determined accurately by controlling the followings: (i) electron–hole pairs are generated, (ii) charge carriers are employed as reactants, and (iii) the photoabsorption characteristics of semiconductor catalyst, and (iv) transmission of charge between catalyst and dye. The absorption-desorption mechanisms help the synthesised photocatalyst absorb more dye in the reaction process. An electron and hole pair creates on the surface level of Co3O4 catalysts by the irradiation of the visible light. The generation of intermediate radicals like hydroxyl radicals takes place within the electron-hole pair of the catalytic solution. Primarily the hydroxyl radicals are triggered into flow by steady air bubbling, as the dye molecules perform an oxidative photodegradation. The photogenerated oxidising radicals easily connect with the absorbed dye molecules in the absorption reaction process concluding the degradation of the dye. The mechanism can be explained by the following reactions (Eqs. 13–19).
(13) Co3O4 (e- + h+) → Co3O4 (e- +e-) (h+ +h+)
(14) h+ + OH- → OH
(15) e- + O2 - →·O2 -
(16) H2O +·O2 → OOH ֗ + OH-
(17) 2OOH ֗ + O2 + H2O2
(18) OH ֗ +·O2 + h+ VB + pollutants → degraded pollutant
(19) OH ֗ +·O2 + h+ VB + degraded pollutant → CO2 + H2O

The peak revealed the degradation of dye on the photocatalytic treatment as the reaction generation increased and the dye band decreased ( Fig. 7a). The disturbance of the chromophore group and the transformation of the dye into trivial molecular weight derivatives correlate with the photocatalytic degradation of the target dye [48]. As a result of the creation of hole and electron (e- and h+), dye degradation occurs on the surface of the catalyst below the irradiation. OH radicals are formed as the combination of water molecules with the holes. In parallel, the O2 scavenges the e- and it is converted into -OH through HOO- and H2O2. An oxidising species -OH degrades the organic molecule (dye) into H2O, CO2, and inorganic ions.

Without affecting the efficiency of degradation of the catalyst (Co3O4 nanoparticles), they were tested for reusability and successfully reused eight times (Fig. 7b & c). 5 mg/ 20 mL and 50 mg/L of the catalyst and initial dye concentration were adopted for the reusability study, this method involved in collecting the nanoparticles of Co3O4 after each cycle using centrifugation process and washed and kept inside an oven for drying at 110 °C for 2 h. The surface area of the catalyst was measured before each cycle. The results for the degradation revealed that a slight efficiency loss occurred after every cycle which is attributed to the slight decrease in the surface area measured by BET (Fig. 7d). After the 8th cycle, the surface area revealed to be decreased by ~15%, and the absorption efficiency was decreased by ~11%. This slight decrease in the absorption capacity is attributable to the decreased surface area of the reused material and/or slight loss of the material during the centrifugal process. the progressive blocking of micropores over an increasing number of cycles could be the reason behind the slight decrease in the surface area. This is attributed to the fact as the number of cycles progressed, the average pore diameter increased, since the fraction of the micropores to macropores declined which is evident from Fig. 7d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the parameters for the photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: Controlled usage of catalysts was investigated throughout the process to limit the excessive usage of catalysts for dye degradation. Various concentrations of Co3O4 were examined by varying its concentration from 5 mg to 20 mg in the same amount of liquid (20 mL) to study the photocatalyst activity on the degradation rate of MBD ( Fig. 8a). The increase in photodegradation rate was observed when the concentration of the Co3O4 is 10 mg or more than that. Further on there was no significant increase in the photodegradation with the increase of Co3O4 concentration above 10 mg which indicates that increasing the optimum loading value, had no significant effect on the degradation percentage as the turbidity is increased by adding the concentration value of catalyst to the dye solution, resists the penetration of light in it [49].

Suspension of Co3O4 in an aqueous solution of MBD was investigated for photocatalytic degradation rate at various concentrations of the MBD ranging from (10–100 mg/ L) for 100 min Fig. 8b represents the studies performed with a fixed quantity of catalyst (5 mg/ 20 mL). From the experiment, it is clearly visible that if the concentration of the dye is increased from 10 m to 100 mg/L the degradation percentage of MB is decreased. The absorption rate of the dye depends on the increase of catalyst surface that influences the concentration of the dye. The percent photodegradation decreases as the free radicals start to attack the dye molecules since the catalyst concentration is constant which makes the number of molecules insufficient. At a higher concentration rate of the dye, the molecules absorb the visible light more significantly than the particles of Co3O4 absorb it, causing a drop in the efficiency rate of the catalytic reaction [50].

As per the literature study, the photocatalytic reaction rate depends on the pH value of the solution. A pH value of 2–9 was investigated with a catalyst amount of (5 mg/20 mL). Sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were used to adjust and balance the pH value in all the experiments. Fig. 8c represents that at a pH value ranging from 6 to 8 has a higher degradation percentage, on the other side the increase or decrease in the pH value affects the percentage of the degradation decrease.

The pH level of the nanoparticles has an impact on the surface charge based on the point of zero charges. It is reported that the pH value for Co3O4 is 7.5 [51]. When the pH value is lower than the point of zero charges, the surface charge is positive, the surface charge is equal at neutral point and at pH value higher than point of zero charge, the surface charge is negative. The photocatalyst surface is charged negatively in alkaline solution and in acidic solution it is positively charged.

The electrostatic interaction is more in the acidic pH, because of that the anionic dye such as MBD is absorbed. The surface charge of the Co3O4 is negative when the pH is more than 7.5 (alkaline). As a result, electrostatic repulsion is experienced by the MBD from the photocatalyst, and this prevents the absorption on the surface of the catalyst and causes the degradation of the anionic dye of MBD. On the other side, the acidic pH range specifically at pH 4.5, the sulphonate group present in the dye combined with H significantly decreases the adsorption rate on the photocatalyst which results to lower percent degradation [52].

At optimised condition, the kinetics of the photocatalytic degradation of MBD is represented in Fig. 8d. Using the first order kinetic model the photodegradation of MBD aqueous solution with Co3O4 in it under visible light is derived. Photocatalytic decolourisation was fitted to the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model by plotting ln (Co/Ct) against the irradiation time (t) which gives a linear model with a correlation constant (R2) of synthesised Co3O4 nanoparticles as 0.99. The calculated rate constant was 0.02335/min. The removal percent of MBD by microrecycled Co3O4 nanoparticles reached ~94.6%. The effect of various experimental parameters, such as initial dye concentration, contact time, and the dose was investigated for this calculation. A significantly high adsorption capacity of 53.87 mg g−1 was achieved in this study.

It is very important to assess the long-term cyclic performance, reusability, and stability of the catalyst. For this, the performance of the Co3O4 for the decomposition of the MBD was evaluated by carrying out cycling experiments for the long term as illustrated in Fig. 9. It is evident from the experiments that the efficiency of the MBD has reduced slightly after the 5th cycle, from 95% to 89%. After the 15th cycle, it reduces to 70%. At the 30th cycle, the efficiency reduced to 52% which is still reasonable compared to the other studies.

Dyeing wastewater treatment methods generally use different techniques which include physical, chemical oxidation, biochemical, and other advanced oxidation processes [53]. The advantages of Co3O4 photocatalytic degradation have been realised by a growing number of researchers and have been studied thoroughly. However, the Co3O4 photocatalyst is expensive and often synthesised by virgin sources which have many environmental and social consequences. In contrast, producing Co3O4 photocatalyst from a problematic waste source of spent LIBs will not only reduce the environmental burden but will also have enormous benefits for the manufacturers. Comparative performance of the efficacy of synthesised catalyst in this study with the other relevant research has been shown in Table 1.

In a previous study, Guo et al. reported the MBD degradation performance of Co3O4 catalyst up to 3 cycles where the degradation rate was reduced from 90.7% to 85.9% in 40 min after 3rd run. In this study, the Co3O4 catalyst was generated from the virgin source of cobalt chloride hexahydrate [54]. In another study, Co3O4 was generated from CoCl2.2 H2O virgin source and utilised for the MBD degradation where the percentage of degradation was 55.71% after 420 min. The researchers did not measure the cyclic stability of the catalyst in this case [55]. Co3O4 nanocubes synthesised from the hydrothermal route with sodium methanesulphonate, employing Co(NO3)2⋅6 H2O and NH3⋅H2O raw materials to degrade Rhodamine B dye (RBD) 82.3% removal was achieved [56]. The researchers did not report the cyclic performance in this study. Co3O4-NP was synthesised by utilising Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate [Co(NO3)2.6 H2O] and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) to degrade methyl orange dye (MOD) where researchers have achieved 83.6% removal after 250 min [57]. The cycle stability was not reported in this study. It is evident from the experiments of the current research that the efficiency of the MBD has reduced slightly after the 5th cycle, from 95% to 89%. After the 15th cycle, it reduces to 70%. At the 30th cycle, the efficiency reduced to 52% which is still reasonable compared to the other studies.

The spent Co3O4 catalyst could be recycled again to recover Co-metal alloys through the oxidation-reduction method utilising carbothermal reduction. The Co3O4 could be mixed with the waste graphite from the anode material or any other waste carbon sources and could be transformed back to CoO and Co metals through high temperature reaction. An approximate technical cost estimation is provided in the supplementary material.

In this research, valuable phase pure Co3O4 nanoparticles (microrecycled product) and Li2CO3 (co-product) were produced. There are plenty of social and environmental benefits to producing Co and Li-based materials from the waste source which could be economically beneficial as well. The primary production of Li has two modes, it requires 250 tonnes of mineral ore spodumene from mining or mineral-rich brine of 750 tonnes to obtain 1 ton of lithium [64,65]. It consumes a large quantity of materials to process in obtaining the final requirement, which creates an environmental impact. The process of obtaining Li from brine solution depletes the water levels, as it is a process of drilling holes in the salt flat for pumping the mineral-rich solution to the surface. In Chile’s Salar de Atacama, one of the major hubs for Li production, mining activities at that region consume more than 65% of water in the region [17]. Farmers from this region are affected by the artificial drought and import water from other regions for farming. Around 1900 tonnes of water is required for extracting 1 ton of Li, consumed by evaporation, which creates a huge demand in the water supply [66]. These processes have a lot of indirect economic impacts.

On the other side, processing for the secondary production type requires 28 tonnes of spent LIBs, to produce 1 ton of Li (roughly 256 spent LIBs from the electric vehicle) [67]. This method helps in creating a requirement for reusing spent LIBs for processing, that minimizes the requirement of extracting Li from the primary sources. Since the growth of the electric automotive field is ever-growing, the supply from only the mineral ores could be devastating.

An immediate requirement for the Co reserve is of utmost importance in the field, these Co reserves are in geographical concentrations (mainly in the politically unstable Democratic Republic of the Congo) [68]. The reserves have recently experienced short-term price fluctuations and various types of social, environmental concerns, and ethical issues around the region of extraction, which employs child labouring in the mines [68]. The researchers found adverse Co duct exposure in the blood and urine of the children in that region due to the cobalt contamination in the soil, water, and air which was correlated with the DNA dagame among the children. It is important to have the coordination for supporting a forward push on the recycling process of LIBs and creating a circular economy of the materials.

Microrecycling technique is proven as a small-scale recycling approach that can be adopted to recycle and remanufacture value-added materials in a refined form. In this research, value added phase pure Co3O4 nanoparticles with a high surface area were synthesised from the hazardous spent LIBs. Under visible light irradiation, MBD is degraded using the synthesised Co3O4 nanoparticles from spent LIBs which is an opportunity to utilise the waste to reduce the waste in the environment. The photocatalytic activity of synthesised Co3O4 NPs was researched for reusability, and it reveals even after using for several cycles the loss is minimal. This gives a scope to use it in large-scale processes as it is economical, feasible, and efficient. The synthesis process has an added benefit to recovering the Li in the form of Li2CO3 as a co-product which has social and environmental advantages.
Authors confirm that this paper is original and has not been published previously and is not considered for publication elsewhere.

Rumana Hossain: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Experiments, Formal analysis. Veena Sahajwalla: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
The research study did not involve any human subjects.

If accepted, the research work will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or any other language, without the Publisher’s written consent. Necessary copyright permission for figures and tables has already been granted for the manuscript.
This research was supported by the Australian Research Council’s Industrial Transformation Research Hub funding scheme (project IH190100009).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2022.107858.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
